title: Candies in a Jar

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: A jar contains 10 RED, 20 BLUE, and 30 GREEN candies. Candies are removed one at a time uniformly at random.
rules: All 60 candies will eventually be removed.
Let T_R, T_B, T_G be the positions (1 through 60) of the LAST candy of each color.
We want P(T_R < T_B AND T_R < T_G) -- the event that ALL reds are gone before the last blue AND before the last green.
setup.question: What is the probability that when all reds are removed, at least one blue and one green remain?

learningObjectives: Decompose into two mutually exclusive cases: T_R < T_B < T_G or T_R < T_G < T_B.
For each case, compute via 'P(last is color X) * P(last among the rest is color Y)'.

stages.label: Decompose into orderings
stages.prompt: We want P(T_R is the smallest of the three). Equivalently, T_R < T_B AND T_R < T_G. There are two mutually exclusive ways for this to happen: ?

options.text: Case 1: T_R < T_B < T_G  (last candy overall is green).  Case 2: T_R < T_G < T_B  (last candy overall is blue).
Only one case: T_R < T_B < T_G.

stages.explanation: T_R must be smallest. The remaining two (T_B, T_G) can be in either order, giving two cases. Sum the disjoint probabilities.

stages.label: P(last candy is GREEN)
stages.prompt: What's P(the very last candy drawn is green)?

options.text: 30/60 = 1/2.  (By symmetry: each candy is equally likely to be drawn last.)

stages.explanation: By symmetry, each of the 60 candies is equally likely to be in position 60. Of those, 30 are green. So P = 30/60 = 1/2.

stages.label: Case 1: P(T_R < T_B < T_G)
stages.prompt: Case 1: green is the last color. Now CONDITION on green being last. We need: among the 30 red+blue candies, the last to appear must be BLUE. P(last red+blue is blue)?

options.text: 20/30  (20 of 30 remaining red+blue are blue, by the same symmetry).  So P(Case 1) = (30/60) * (20/30) = 20/60 = 1/3.

stages.explanation: The 30 red+blue candies' relative order is independent of green's positioning. Among them, P(last is blue) = 20/30 by symmetry. So P(Case 1) = (1/2) * (2/3) = 1/3.

stages.label: Case 2: P(T_R < T_G < T_B)
stages.prompt: Case 2: blue is last. P(last candy is blue) = 20/60 = 1/3. Among 40 red+green, P(last is green) = ?
stages.explanation: P(last is blue) = 20/60 = 1/3. Then P(last red+green is green) = 30/40 = 3/4. Product: 1/4.

stages.label: Commit total probability
stages.prompt: P(T_R < T_B AND T_R < T_G) = P(Case 1) + P(Case 2) = 1/3 + 1/4 = ?
stages.explanation: 1/3 + 1/4 = 4/12 + 3/12 = 7/12 ≈ 0.5833. So with probability 7/12, all reds are removed before either blue or green is exhausted.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Verify by an alternative method: P(R is removed first as a color) = ? Why does this differ from 7/12?
Generalize: with R red, B blue, G green, what's P(red color is exhausted first)?
If you wanted to know P(red AND blue both exhausted before green is finished), how would the calculation change?
